Q1.bottleneck: Already highly optimized - beating DuckDB by 2x
Q1.root_cause: Parallel hash aggregation with thread-local tables is working very well. Only 4 groups means minimal merge overhead. The scan+filter+aggregate pipeline is efficient.
Q1.benchmark_gap: Q1 is 43ms vs DuckDB 83-90ms. GenDB is 2x FASTER than DuckDB. Further optimization has diminishing returns.

Q3.bottleneck: Hash aggregation still uses std::unordered_map instead of robin_hood. Excessive data materialization during joins. Zone map checks are inside parallel loops (redundant work).
Q3.root_cause: Q3AggTable is defined as std::unordered_map<Q3GroupKey, double, Q3GroupKeyHash> in index.h. This is 2-5x slower than robin_hood for 300K groups. Additionally, OrderMatch and LineitemMatch structs are being created and copied into vectors, materializing ~1.2M intermediate results. Zone map skip logic is checked per-row instead of pre-computed once.
Q3.benchmark_gap: Q3 is 352ms vs DuckDB 81-90ms. GenDB is 4.3x SLOWER than DuckDB. This is the critical bottleneck - closing this gap should be the top priority.

Q6.bottleneck: Already highly optimized - beating DuckDB by 1.7x
Q6.root_cause: Parallel scan with zone maps is working excellently. Simple scalar aggregation (single SUM) across threads is very efficient. Zone maps skip 86% of blocks as designed.
Q6.benchmark_gap: Q6 is 12ms vs DuckDB 20ms. GenDB is 1.7x FASTER than DuckDB. Further optimization has diminishing returns.

analysis.overall: GenDB is already beating DuckDB on Q1 and Q6 by 1.7-2x, which is excellent. The critical bottleneck is Q3 (352ms vs 81-90ms target), which needs a 4x speedup to match DuckDB. The main issues are: (1) Q3 aggregation hash table still uses std::unordered_map instead of robin_hood, (2) excessive intermediate data materialization during joins, (3) zone map overhead from redundant per-row checks. Focus all optimization effort on Q3.

performance_optimizations.technique: Replace std::unordered_map with robin_hood for Q3 aggregation
performance_optimizations.description: In index.h line 3, Q3AggTable is defined as std::unordered_map<Q3GroupKey, double, Q3GroupKeyHash>. Change this to robin_hood::unordered_flat_map<Q3GroupKey, double, Q3GroupKeyHash>. The robin_hood implementation is already included and used elsewhere (hash joins, Q1 aggregation), but Q3's aggregation was missed. With ~300K groups after join, robin_hood's superior cache locality and probing strategy should provide 2-3x speedup on the aggregation phase. Also apply robin_hood to all thread-local Q3AggTable instances (thread_agg_tables vector in q3.cpp).
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: 2-3x speedup on Q3 aggregation phase (estimated 50-100ms savings). Q3 spends significant time building and probing the aggregation hash table with 300K groups. Robin hood's open addressing with backward shift deletion has much better cache behavior than std::unordered_map's chaining. Expected total Q3 time: 352ms → 250-300ms.

performance_optimizations.description: In q3.cpp, the code creates OrderMatch and LineitemMatch structs, copies them into thread-local vectors (thread_order_matches, thread_lineitem_matches), then merges those vectors. This materializes ~375K OrderMatch structs + ~1.2M LineitemMatch structs = ~1.6M structs worth of copies. Instead: (1) After the customer⋈orders join, directly build the hash table for lineitem join without creating OrderMatch vector. Stream filtered orders directly into the hash table builder. (2) For lineitem join, eliminate LineitemMatch struct. Instead, directly aggregate into thread-local Q3AggTable during the lineitem probe loop. This fuses join+aggregation into a single pass. No intermediate vectors needed. This is operator fusion - scan lineitem, filter by date, probe join, aggregate, all in one tight loop per thread.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Eliminates ~1.6M struct allocations and copies. Reduces memory traffic by ~50-100MB. Improves cache locality by keeping hot data (aggregation hash table) in L3 cache instead of spilling intermediate vectors. Estimated 40-80ms savings. Expected Q3 time after optimization 1+2: 352ms → 200-250ms.

performance_optimizations.technique: Pre-compute zone map skip ranges once instead of per-row checks
performance_optimizations.description: In q3.cpp lines 99-108 and 199-208, zone maps are used to create skip_orders_block and skip_lineitem_block boolean vectors. However, lines 120-126 and 219-226 check these vectors for EVERY row inside the parallel loop. For a 65K-row block, this means 65K redundant checks of the same boolean value. Instead: pre-compute skip ranges (start_row, end_row) for each contiguous region of skippable blocks. Then in the parallel loop, use binary search or range checks to skip entire regions at once. For example, if blocks 10-50 are all skippable (representing rows 327680-1638400 for orders with 32K block size), jump i directly to row 1638400 instead of checking the boolean 1,310,720 times.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Eliminates ~1M boolean checks per query. Reduces branch mispredictions. Estimated 10-20ms savings. This is a smaller win than optimizations 1-2 but compounds with them. Expected Q3 time after all 3: 352ms → 180-230ms.
performance_optimizations.knowledge_source: novel approach (optimization of existing zone map implementation)

performance_optimizations.description: In q1.cpp line 25, the filter checks if (lineitem.l_shipdate[i] <= cutoff_date). This is a scalar comparison executed 60M times. Use AVX2 to compare 8 int32_t dates at once. The filter is inside the aggregation loop, so we can't easily separate it, but we can vectorize the comparison within the loop body. Create a SIMD version of the aggregation function that processes 8 rows at a time: load 8 shipdates, compare with cutoff, create a mask, then conditionally aggregate the passing rows. The CPU has AVX2 support (verified via lscpu). Expected speedup: 2-3x on the filter portion. Since Q1 is already very fast (43ms) and filter selectivity is 98%, the absolute time savings will be modest.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Q1 filter represents ~5-10ms of the 43ms total (rough estimate - most time is in aggregation and memory access). SIMD could reduce this to 2-4ms, saving 5-8ms total. Expected Q1 time: 43ms → 35-38ms. Diminishing returns since Q1 is already 2x faster than DuckDB.

performance_optimizations.description: Q6 has 4 predicates: l_shipdate >= date_start, l_shipdate < date_end, l_discount >= 0.05, l_discount <= 0.07, l_quantity < 24. Currently evaluated in scalar mode (one row at a time). Use AVX2 to evaluate 8 rows at once: (1) Load 8 shipdates, compare with bounds, (2) Load 8 discounts, compare with bounds, (3) Load 8 quantities, compare with threshold, (4) AND the masks together, (5) For passing rows, load extended_price and discount, compute revenue, accumulate. The parallel_scan_aggregate function in q6.cpp would need a SIMD-optimized variant. The CPU has AVX2 support.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Q6 is already very fast (12ms) and highly selective (zone maps skip 86% of blocks, so only ~8.4M rows are scanned instead of 60M). SIMD can speed up the 4-predicate evaluation on those 8.4M rows. Estimated 2-3x speedup on filter evaluation. Since filter is ~50% of the 12ms (rest is memory access and aggregation), savings are ~3-5ms. Expected Q6 time: 12ms → 7-9ms. Again, diminishing returns.

summary: GenDB is already outperforming DuckDB on Q1 (2x faster) and Q6 (1.7x faster), demonstrating excellent parallelism and zone map effectiveness. The critical bottleneck is Q3 (4.3x slower than DuckDB at 352ms vs 81-90ms target). Priority 1-3 optimizations target Q3 with high-impact, low-risk changes: (1) Fix the missed robin_hood migration for Q3 aggregation hash table (2-3x speedup), (2) Eliminate intermediate join result materialization via operator fusion (40-80ms savings), (3) Optimize zone map skip logic to avoid redundant per-row checks (10-20ms savings). These three changes together could bring Q3 down to 180-230ms, reducing the DuckDB gap from 4.3x to 2-2.5x. Priorities 4-5 target Q1/Q6 with SIMD, but these have diminishing returns since those queries are already beating DuckDB. Focus on Q3 first.
